summary: I was born in Chickasha, Oklahoma, in 1919, on a very cold, wintry night during a blizzard. Chickasha was a small town just south of Oklahoma City, mostly agricultural at the time, with many Indian tribes centered there after their removal from the South. I grew up there as a small-town boy, spending time both in town and on farms with my relatives. It wasn’t an oil town back then, although oil was discovered there in later years.

Oklahoma City was the larger city just north of Chickasha, where I was born and raised. Although I lived in Chickasha, I often thought of Oklahoma City as the nearby urban center. My family’s life in Chickasha was influenced by its proximity to Oklahoma City, but my upbringing was very much rooted in the smaller town environment.

My father was born in Texas and later moved to Indian Territory with his parents. He rode up from Texas on a horse as a young man before settling in Oklahoma. His roots in Texas were an important part of his early life before he became a teacher and businessman in Indian Territory.

Indian Territory was where my family settled after coming from Texas and North Carolina. Chickasha was part of Indian Territory when I was born, and it was home to many Indian tribes who had been removed from the South. My father taught school there, and it was a place of both cultural significance and small-town life during my childhood.

My mother came from North Carolina a few years before meeting my father in Indian Territory. She was my father’s pupil when he was teaching school, which is how they met. Her background from North Carolina brought a different perspective to our family life in Oklahoma.
